bias: Left outlets frame the deployment as dangerous escalation—using terms like "Panicked Pentagon ," "major escalation," and experts calling the MEU a "key indicator" of a possible ground operation—while right outlets frame it as decisive deterrence, stressing "protect the Strait of Hormuz," references to a "Trump Admin...Orders" posture, and hawkish phrases such as "no mercy, no quarter.
The left amplifies higher counts ; the right foregrounds ~2,500 as proof of resolve.
Center coverage cautiously highlights MEU crisis‑response roles, not invasion.
The pivotal divide is invasion versus crisis‑response; both agree forces moved amid rising Iran tensions and rely on anonymous officials.
summary: On Friday, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth approved a U.S. Central Command request to deploy roughly 2,500 Marines and the Japan-based USS Tripoli amphibious assault ship to the Middle East as part of the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit.

After Iran intensified missile and drone attacks on the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting global shipping nearly two weeks into the conflict, CENTCOM requested the amphibious reinforcement to expand operational options.

U.S. and Israeli strikes have hit roughly 6,000 targets in Iran, while U.S. forces have sustained 13 service members killed and roughly 140 wounded. The 31st MEU includes about 1,100 ground combat Marines and supporting logistics units.

Officials say the deployment expands commanders' military options without committing to ground invasion. Quinnipiac polling shows 53% of registered voters oppose military action while 40% approve, with 74% fearing rising energy prices.

Sailing from Sasebo, Japan, the USS Tripoli remains over a week away from Iranian waters, accompanied by a combat logistics battalion capable of sustaining operations for up to 15 days amid reported contingency planning for raids or seizing Kharg Island.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left outlets frame Grenell’s exit as proof of institutional damage, using charged words like "disastrous," "crony," and hyperbole such as "burning the place to the ground" while foregrounding artist boycotts; right outlets portray a managerial victory—branding the "Trump Kennedy Center," praising "tremendous progress" and using soft verbs like "stepping down" or "appointed" to emphasize renovation plans and operational competence; pivotal divides are artist departures, renaming/branding, and the board’s rationale; center coverage is more procedural; both sides agree Grenell is leaving, Trump named a successor, and a two‑year closure is planned, reflecting cultural and political rifts.
summary: On Friday, President Donald Trump announced that Richard Grenell will transition out of his role as president of the Kennedy Center, a position he held since his appointment last year.
Grenell served as a central player in President Trump's push to dramatically overhaul the Kennedy Center shortly after he returned to office, filling the board with his supporters.
The Kennedy Center will close this summer for a two-year reconstruction, following the board's decision to rename it the Trump Kennedy Center, a move the Kennedy family denounced.
Matt Floca, the vice president of facilities operations, will succeed Grenell as chief operating officer and executive director, with the change formalized at Monday's White House board meeting.

Many artists have withdrawn from the Kennedy Center since the leadership transition, as Trump continues targeting cultural institutions to remove what he calls liberal bias and anti-American ideology.
title: Trump ally Ric Grenell stepping down as Kennedy Center president, sources say

bias: Left coverage frames the ruling as a judicial rebuke protecting Fed independence, foregrounding verbs like "quashes" and moral terms such as "retribution" and "harass," while right outlets portray the same decision as partisan obstruction, using charged labels like "activist judge," "throws out," "protects," or sarcastic "President Boasberg" and amplifying outrage over a purportedly blocked investigation; the clearest dividing lines are motive and the judge’s role .
Center pieces add procedural detail .

Both sides nonetheless repeat the judge's "essentially zero evidence" finding and note the DOJ appeal, reflecting shared attention to legal stakes even as interpretations split along defense-of-institutions versus defense-of-prosecutorial-authority lines.
summary: On Friday, Chief Judge James Boasberg in Washington quashed grand jury subpoenas targeting Chairman Jerome Powell , finding the investigation by Attorney Jeanine Pirro lacked factual basis.

Boasberg concluded the subpoenas served as a pretext to pressure the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates, after President Trump and his deputies made at least 100 public attacks against the Chair.
In his March 11 ruling unsealed on Friday, Chief Judge Boasberg wrote, "The Government has offered no evidence whatsoever that Powell committed any crime other than displeasing the President."

Attorney Pirro opened the probe to examine whether Powell gave false testimony to Congress about a building renovation, which the Chair previously described as a campaign to undermine central bank independence.
Trump repeatedly pressured the Federal Reserve on social media, calling Powell "TOO LATE" and "TOO POLITICAL" while demanding his ouster when interest rate cuts failed to materialize.
title: Judge blocks subpoenas in criminal probe of Fed Chair Jerome Powell

bias: Left coverage treats the revised 0.7% Q4 GDP as a "big downgrade" and "sluggish" growth, highlighting household strain and the 43‑day shutdown as hobbles and grounds for political blame; right coverage emphasizes PCE inflation , using language like "stubbornly high," "just," or "cut in half" to amplify policy and market risks while de‑emphasizing partisan culpability; center pieces stress "sticky inflation" and Fed implications.
All sides agree on the revision, inflation pressure, and the shutdown’s role—differences map onto disputes over political accountability versus monetary‑policy priorities.
summary: The Commerce Department's second estimate shows fourth-quarter GDP at a 0.7% annualized rate, down from the initial 1.4% and below 4.4% in the third quarter.
The 43-day government shutdown last fall and a 16.7% plunge in federal government spending and investment reduced fourth-quarter growth, the Commerce Department reported Friday.
The Commerce Department on Friday reported that the personal consumption expenditures index rose 0.3% in January and is up 2.8% from a year ago, while core PCE increased 0.4% and 3.1% annually.
Federal Reserve officials are watching inflation and geopolitical risks as the U.S. labor market weakens and 2025 GDP slows to 2.1%, influencing potential policy decisions.

Rising energy costs tied to the Iran war have pushed up oil and gas prices, exports fell 3.3% in the fourth quarter, and business investment excluding housing rose 2.2%, with the final GDP report due April 9.
title: US fourth-quarter GDP revised down to 0.7% growth; January core inflation at 3.1%

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the crash as part of a "mounting cost" of conflict, emphasizing "140 wounded" and the "human tragedy" to critique military escalation.

Conversely, right-leaning coverage portrays the deceased as "American Heroes," using religious appeals like "say a prayer" to honor their "tragic loss" while highlighting the "15,000 targets" hit as a strategic win.

While the center focuses on "operational reliability" and the aging fleet, both sides acknowledge the "incident" was not from hostile fire, yet they remain divided by a tension between highlighting the "frenzy" of war versus prioritizing national security objectives.
summary: On Thursday, March 12, 2026, a U.S. Air Force KC-135 Stratotanker crashed in western Iraq during Operation Epic Fury, killing all six crew members, U.S. Central Command confirmed on Friday.
The crash resulted from an unspecified incident involving two aircraft in friendly airspace, with the second plane landing safely; CENTCOM officials ruled out hostile or friendly fire as causes.

Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of Iranian proxies, claimed responsibility for downing the aircraft "in defense of our country's sovereignty," though U.S. officials state there is no evidence supporting the claim.

This incident marks the fourth U.S. military aircraft loss during Operation Epic Fury, immediately straining CENTCOM's mid-air refuelling capacity and reducing availability for sustained air operations against Iranian targets.
The aging KC-135 fleet, in service for over 60 years, faces reliability concerns as the Air Force transitions to next-generation KC-46A Pegasus tankers to replace the outdated aircraft.
title: US military confirms all six crew members dead after refueling plane crashed in Iraq

bias: Left coverage frames the strikes as escalation and presidential showmanship—using critiques like "showmanship" and "flips" and warning that threatening oil infrastructure risks retaliation—while right outlets celebrate a decisive military victory, deploying triumphant terms such as "obliterated," "historic," "crown jewel," or "smashes" and portraying sparing oil facilities as "for reasons of decency.
Pivotal divides include threats to oil infrastructure and calls for independent verification and Iranian responses .
Centers stress troop figures and market/retaliation risks; all agree the president announced strikes on Kharg Island and oil sites were spared for now.
summary: On March 14, 2026, President Donald Trump said U.S. forces totally obliterated Kharg Island's military targets after United States Central Command executed a major bombing raid.
Washington has escalated with over 5,000 targets struck, ordering 2,500 Marines and additional naval ships to West Asia amid nearly two weeks of war.

Military officials said US Air Force bombers struck missile storage sites and facilities housing mines, adding economic infrastructure was not targeted while the Navy will soon escort commercial tankers through the Strait of Hormuz.
Regionally, strikes coincide with casualties, including one in Baghdad and at least 12 medical staff in southern Lebanon, affecting regional stability amid oil market uncertainty.

The New York Times reported that a preliminary military investigation found a U.S. missile likely destroyed an Iranian elementary school, while Pete Hegseth, US Defence Secretary, said the incident is under investigation.
title: Trump says U.S. ‘obliterated’ military targets on Iran’s Kharg Island but didn’t ‘wipe out’ oil infrastructure

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes obstruction and legal-access stakes, foregrounding the VAAP and Jill Martin Diaz and using adversarial phrasing like "impeding their work" while stressing reliance on "phones and computers" for interpretation; right-leaning pieces frame a policy clash over rule changes, using terms such as "accuse" and "clash/clashing" to underscore enforcement concerns; center reporting highlights a "new commissioner" as context.

Detainee perspectives and a Corrections Department response are de-emphasized across outlets, yet all report the VAAP allegation and device restrictions—a common thread that maps onto an access‑versus‑order divide.
summary: This fall, VAAP attorneys said access to immigration detainees at Vermont prisons has become harder since Jon Murad became interim Corrections Commissioner, citing language barriers and limited meeting space.
Department officials say they enforced long-standing policies, with Murad denying accusations he aimed to impede legal work, asserting policies predate his tenure.

In September the department stopped providing attorneys with detainees' alien registration numbers, and banning personal devices in late October led to VAAP attorneys relying on one shared landline, reducing meetings to 25% of detainees.
A memo from legislators demands a memorandum of understanding between DOC and VAAP to guarantee cooperation as attorneys warn of 'irreparable harm' to vulnerable community members.

In January the department evaluated interpretation services and began issuing interpretation tablets for attorneys, and Amanda Wheeler, Gov. Phil Scott's press secretary, said Scott stands by Murad and noted recent positive feedback from VAAP.
title: Immigration lawyers accuse Vermont prisons of impeding their work

bias: Left outlets emphasize political culpability and medical vulnerability—using terms like "ultra‑right" and "attempted coup" and citing a prior stabbing—while right outlets humanize Bolsonaro, highlighting imprisonment and family appeals with words like "rushed," "feeling bad," "pray," and naming the prison "Papudinha.
Center coverage de‑emphasizes politicized language and stresses clinical detail such as "tests confirmed" bronchopneumonia of probable "aspiration.

Pivotal dividing lines are prior health history, custody context, and medical causation; all sides nonetheless report ICU admission, bronchopneumonia, symptoms and the 27‑year sentence, reflecting deeper ideological and cultural rifts.
summary: On Friday, President Jair Bolsonaro was admitted to Star Hospital in Brasilia for intensive care treatment of pneumonia, high fever, chills, and low oxygen levels.
Bolsonaro is currently serving a 27-year sentence for plotting a coup after losing the 2022 presidential election to President Luiz Inacio Lula.
Earlier on Friday, his son stated on X that Bolsonaro was transferred from prison after waking with chills and vomiting; medical exams confirmed aspiration-related bronchopneumonia.
Bolsonaro, who governed Brazil from 2019 to 2022, has a history of hospitalizations stemming from a 2018 campaign stabbing, plus recent head trauma and hernia procedures.
Despite convictions for leading an armed criminal organization, Bolsonaro intends to run for president this year, with recent polls showing him nearly tied with Lula.
title: Brazil's jailed ex-president Bolsonaro taken to hospital after feeling ill

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the visa revocation as retaliatory—using 'tit-for-tat' and phrases like 'prohibited from visiting' while naming Darren Beattie as the denied visitor—whereas right-leaning copy emphasizes formal controls and conditional diplomacy, invoking terms like 'government agent' and noting that Minister Alexandre de Moraes 'signed a visit' and linking entry to exchanges involving Trump or Padilla; center coverage de-emphasizes partisan labels, calling it a 'reciprocal measure' and supplying prior U.S. visa context .
All sides agree the move related to a planned Bolsonaro prison visit and was framed by Lula as reciprocal.
summary: On Friday, Brazil revoked the visa of U.S. State Department official, calling it a reciprocal measure after U.S. visa restrictions last year, Lula said.

On Thursday, Justice Alexandre de Moraes denied Beattie access to the Brasilia prison and noted his visa request to attend the Brazil–US Critical Minerals Forum in São Paulo on Wednesday, citing potential 'undue interference'.

An anonymous Brazilian official told The Associated Press the visa was revoked due to 'the omission of information and lies about the purpose of the visit upon his visa request', and The White House declined to comment.
Officials noted this may complicate Lula's plans to meet Trump this month, tied to an August visa move by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
Beyond the immediate dispute, Lula's move may strain Brazil–U.S. relations as he campaigns for reelection later this year against Sen. Flávio Bolsonaro, with no set date for talks with Trump.
title: Brazil revokes visa of US diplomat who sought to visit Bolsonaro in prison

bias: Coverage splits: left frames the change as an accountability story—using terms like "hastily constructed," reporting a measles outbreak, internal documents and a reported $1.2 billion contract to suggest systemic failure—while right frames it as a managerial fix, emphasizing ICE "replaces contractor" and the new firm is "tasked with improving medical care," de-emphasizing outbreaks and contract figures.
Center reportage sits between, amplifying detainee descriptions of conditions as "inhumane" and calling the incoming contractor "more experienced.
Pivotal divides are public‑health details, contract dollar/context, and detainee testimony; all agree ICE is swapping firms to improve services, reflecting accountability versus order priorities.
summary: An internal ICE document distributed this week shows U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement is preparing to terminate its $1.2 billion contract for Camp East Montana.
Earlier this year, health and safety problems at Camp East Montana emerged, with at least 14 measles cases detected, about 1,500 detainees remaining, and 60 federal detention standards violated.
Built in weeks beside Fort Bliss, the camp features enormous white tents with open-top pods and contract language stating roughly two-week stays, while detainees and advocates alleged abuse.

The Department of Homeland Security is reviewing the Camp East Montana contract with no decisions made, while Amentum Services Inc. won a separate contract for housing, medical care and transportation.
The move comes as ICE deaths have risen, with at least 30 last year and nine this year, amid scrutiny over detention expansion and DHS's $1.2 billion warehouse plan.
title: ICE replaces contractor at largest detention camp after scrutiny of living conditions

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes alleged police culpability—using phrases like "wrongful death," "Taser death," "shocked him multiple times" and stressing the unsealing of "bodycam video" to demand accountability and foreground the family's plan to appeal—while right-leaning pieces foreground the judge's action, repeatedly using "dismissed" and naming the ruling to frame a legal-resolution, law-and-order narrative that de-emphasizes the family's reaction; center pieces take a procedural tone, avoiding emotive language.

Pivotal divides include the family's appeal, limited medical/autopsy detail, and the legal reasoning for dismissal; all sides nonetheless agree the suit was dismissed, the Taser was used repeatedly, and footage was unsealed, reflecting broader debates over institutional trust versus scrutiny.
summary: U.S. District Court Judge Kristi K. DuBose dismissed a wrongful-death suit on Tuesday, deeming the force objectively reasonable, and unsealed body-camera footage previously unreleased.
Officers approached Dallas after responding to a report of possible burglary and trespassing at a trailer park, then demanded he exit the car and pulled him from it, sparking a scuffle.
The medical examiner found three abrasions and six punctures, four consistent with deeper Taser probe injuries, and concluded Dallas died mainly from mixed drug toxicity including methamphetamine.
His family announced plans to take the case to the U.S. 11th Circuit Court of Appeals after protests in Mobile, following Dallas's death on July 2, 2023.
An expert hired by the Dallas family said he was reacting to the Taser's burning pain, and bodycam audio captures 'Please, please, I can't breathe' and 'That's illegal'.
title: Alabama judge dismisses lawsuit over a 2023 Taser death as bodycam video is unsealed

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the bill as a legitimizing, science-focused inquiry—using words like "study," "hear him out," and even the optimistic phrase "the truth is out there"—emphasizing potential benefits and a state research center, while right-leaning coverage foregrounds cost and skepticism, spotlighting "requests, $300,000," asserting "taxpayers to fund," and deploying incredulous tones that mock the proposal as wasteful; centrist pieces pose the question and de-emphasize judgment.
The pivot points are funding size, creation of a state center, and whether UAPs merit government study; both sides still report the bill, UConn’s role, and the legislative hearing.
summary: On March 12, the Appropriations Committee reviewed H.B. 5422, which would require UConn to study UAPs and deliver a report by July 1, 2027.
Amid growing calls, Hoxha said a dedicated program could record, study and collect UAP data, with bipartisan backing from Bumgardner, who cited security reasons.

Funding estimates range from $150,000 for a limited project to roughly $300,000 for broader effort, with University of Connecticut warning a meaningful study requires dedicated funds amid budget constraints.
Legal and confidentiality limits mean some agency records may be inaccessible to non-law-enforcement researchers, Commissioner Ronnell Higgins said, urging removal of the bill's data-sharing mandate.

National-Level attention to UAPs grew after a 2022 congressional hearing and the DoD's All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office was established, while critics question if a state-funded study best serves competing priorities.
title: Should Connecticut Study UFOs? State Rep. Asks Legislature to Hear Him Out

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the subway shoves as apparently random, emphasizes victims’ ages and a swift police response—highlighting the arrest after authorities "tracked down" a suspect at a homeless shelter—while de-emphasizing immigration/deportation details.

Right-Leaning coverage foregrounds immigration, citing government claims of "four-time deported," uses charged labels like "illegal alien" or "thug," and personalizes harm by naming an "Air Force veteran" to underscore enforcement failures.
Center outlets favor legalistic language .
Both sides align on the shoving, arrests, and charges, revealing deeper divides over immigration and public-safety priorities.
summary: On Tuesday, authorities arrested 34-year-old Bairon Posada-Hernandez in Brooklyn for allegedly pushing 30-year-old Jhon Rodriguez and 83-year-old Air Force veteran Richard Williams onto subway tracks Sunday at the Lexington Avenue-63rd Street station.
The Department of Homeland Security confirmed Posada-Hernandez is a Honduran national who first entered the U.S. on January 2, 2008, and has been deported four times, most recently in 2020.

Williams remains in critical condition with severe brain injuries after his head struck the tracks; Rodriguez is recovering from shoulder and neck injuries after both victims were pulled to safety before a train arrived.

DHS Deputy Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis demanded that New York officials cooperate with an ICE detainer, stating, "DHS is calling upon New York sanctuary politicians to commit to this ICE detainer and not release this heinous criminal back into New York communities."
NYPD data shows nine subway push incidents so far in 2026, up from 19 in 2025 and 26 in 2024, reflecting an emerging public safety concern in the transit system.
title: Man charged in NY subway shove deported 4 times: DHS

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the verdict as institutional accountability—foregrounding words like "guilty," "manslaughter," "killed" and repeatedly "rammed," and stressing the defendant remained free on bail and "faces a maximum of five to 15 years"—to amplify moral outrage and consequence; right-leaning pieces use terser legal language, de-emphasize the victim's name, sometimes highlight a "high-speed chase" or the trooper's age, and frame it as an individual criminal matter; center coverage is process-focused and names the victim, even using evocative terms like "crushed.

The biggest divides are victim identification and sentencing/bail emphasis; all agree on the conviction and prosecutors' account, reflecting deeper ideological splits over institutional accountability versus individual culpability.
summary: On Friday, the former New York state trooper was convicted of manslaughter at his second trial, which began last month.

On Dec. 22, 2020, Baldner pulled Tristin Goods over for speeding near Kingston, and after an argument including pepper-spray, Goods drove off, prompting a pursuit while his family headed north from New York City.
Defense attorneys said the SUV cut off Baldner and collided twice, and an expert testified Goods lost control after overcorrecting following a minor impact.
The crash claimed the life of an 11-year-old passenger, Monica Goods, who was in the SUV while the Goods family headed north from New York City to visit family.
A prior jury in November acquitted Baldner of murder and reckless endangerment but deadlocked on manslaughter, prompting the retrial; sentencing is scheduled for June 2.
title: Jury finds ex-NY trooper guilty of manslaughter in 2020 chase that killed 11-year-old

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as an identity-driven clash—emphasizing "Hispanic resolutions," portraying the episode as a "bitter" internal fight, highlighting that the lawmaker was "excluded," named, fined $300 and "threatened" another—casting it as party discipline and marginalization.
Right-Leaning coverage focuses narrowly on the guilty plea and a "feud," de-emphasizing name, fine and phone-call details and framing it as routine misconduct or an identity- politics squabble.
Center outlets present legal facts with some "feud" language.
All perspectives agree on the guilty plea and that this was a dispute rather than a major criminal case, reflecting divides over identity versus institutional framing.
summary: On Friday, State Rep. Sylvia Ortiz-Velez pleaded guilty in Milwaukee County, and Judge Paul Malloy ordered her to pay a $300 fine and submit a DNA sample.
Amid resolution drafting, Ortiz-Velez believed an unnamed lawmaker excluded her from working on resolutions honoring Hispanic heritage and Hispanic veterans, then contacted media outlets.

According to the criminal complaint, some of Ortiz-Velez's remarks were 'indecent and tended to disrupt the good public order', Democratic leaders said she mentioned 'shooting three caucus members', and investigators were told she threatened to spread 'negative personal information' about the resolutions' author.

Prosecutors in Milwaukee County charged State Rep. Sylvia Ortiz-Velez in February, and under the statute she could have faced up to 90 days in jail, though she denied threatening colleagues in interviews with Wisconsin Right Now and the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel.

Her attorney Michael Cernin said Assembly Democrats were already upset because Ortiz-Velez’s voting on the 2025-27 state budget and legislative maps caused a rift that has been ugly and bitter, while a spokesperson for Assembly Republican Speaker Robin Vos said she shouldn’t have been banned.
title: Wisconsin Legislator Pleads Guilty to Disorderly Conduct in Feud over Hispanic Resolutions

bias: Left outlets frame the fee cut—from $2,350 to $450, an ~80% reduction—as a hard‑won victory for Americans abroad, stressing advocacy, "campaign" and relief words like "finally" and people-focused phrasing such as "give up citizenship.

Right outlets foreground individual liberty and administrative rollback with terse, headline-style terms like "cuts," "renounce US citizenship," and heavy numeric emphasis to portray a win for personal choice.
The key divides are advocacy/context and regional mentions versus concise policy framing; both agree on the core facts, reflecting broader ideological and cultural priorities.
summary: The State Department reduced the fee to renounce U.S. citizenship from $2,350 to $450, an approximately 80% cut, effective Friday as published in the Federal Register.
This fee reduction returns the cost to the 2010 level when fees for citizenship renunciation first began after a prior increase in 2015.
The reduction follows years of legal challenges by groups including the Association of Accidental Americans, who argued that the fee was too high and should be lower or free.

Fabien Lahagre, president of the Association of Accidental Americans, called the fee cut a victory resulting from six years of legal advocacy and said it makes the right to renounce citizenship more accessible to all.
title: State Department Slashes Fee to Renounce US Citizenship by 80% to $450

summary: The procurement memo dated Feb. 25 asks the federal government to prepare the Marana facility to hold 775 detainees, naming Management & Training Corporation as the likely operator.
Following Congress's $45 billion for detention, DHS/ICE says it needs more beds to meet interior enforcement and border 'decompression' goals, the procurement notice reads.
The Marana facility was a former state prison with a documented capacity of 513 and was operated as a minimum-security prison until 2023, according to state records.

Marana officials say the town was not a party to the federal-MTC agreement and that details have not been finalized, while local advocates warn raising capacity would be devastating for detained people.

Advocates raise concern about detainee welfare and oversight as the ACLU notes there are no enforceable standards amid detention system growth in recent years, while deaths in custody peaked in Fiscal Year 2025.
title: Federal order seeks major capacity increase at proposed Arizona ICE detention center

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the $10 billion TikTok deal as a "commission" for government "intermediation," using words like "fee" and "brokering" to imply ethical concerns or administrative profiteering.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage portrays the transaction as a "strategic win" to "save" the app, emphasizing "strong leadership" and "securing U.S. data.
While the right highlights the "U.S. Treasury" as the recipient, the left de-emphasizes this, focusing on "businessmen" linked to the president.
Center-Leaning reports typically maintain neutral descriptions, though some adopt hyperbolic terms like "MAMMOTH windfall.
Both sides agree on the $10 billion figure, reflecting deep-seated ideological divides.
summary: On March 13, Reuters reported the Trump administration is set to receive a roughly $10 billion fee from investors in the deal for TikTok's U.S. business , the Wall Street Journal said.
ByteDance in January finalized a deal to create TikTok USDS Joint Venture LLC, a majority American-owned joint venture securing U.S. user data, though few divestiture details were disclosed.
Investors including Oracle, Silver Lake and Abu Dhabi's MGX paid about $2.5 billion upfront to the U.S. Treasury Department and will make further payments until $10 billion is reached.

Legal challenges followed the deal, as retail investors sued President Trump and U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi earlier this month seeking to reverse the approval, officials said the fee is justified.
The deal affects over 200 million Americans and Vice President JD Vance said the new U.S. company is valued around $14 billion, with coverage including a 3D-printed Trump model.
title: Trump Administration Set to Receive $10 Billion Fee for Brokering TikTok Deal

bias: Left coverage frames the two executive orders chiefly as a politically timed move—using phrases like "ahead of midterms" to suggest electoral motive—while right coverage foregrounds bold deregulatory wins, with language such as "slash red tape," "fighting the housing crisis," and "loosens restrictions" to cast the actions as decisive benefits .

Rhetorically the left adopts skeptical timing and intent, the right uses urgent, action-oriented praise; the center emphasizes mechanics—"removing unnecessary regulatory barriers" and implementation details—revealing a pivotal divide over motive versus means.
All sides, however, agree the orders target home affordability and mortgage-related barriers.
summary: On Friday, President Donald Trump signed two executive orders directing federal agencies to ease housing regulations and the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to expand mortgage lending before November's elections.

Housing pressures driven by construction shortfalls and higher rates have made homes less affordable, while on Thursday the Senate passed a bipartisan housing bill supported by the White House on March 2.

Federal agencies are ordered to update regulations slowing homebuilding, curtail some 'green' mandates, direct the EPA and Secretary of the Army to review water permits, and simplify historic-review guidance aligning tax credits with Opportunity Zone breaks.

The administration expects loosening mortgage rules to boost lender competition and lower borrowing costs, with prospective homebuyers seeing effects within months amid a $398,000 February median existing-home price and 6.05% average 30-year mortgage rate in February.
The plan creates tradeoffs between faster construction and environmental protections, with outcomes uncertain due to state and local policies and mortgage rate changes, officials say.
title: Trump signs executive orders aimed at addressing home affordability concerns ahead of midterms

bias: Left outlets frame the proposal with skeptical, civil‑liberties language—repeating "wants to build" and terming an "underground center" part of an "overhaul"—emphasizing secrecy and public‑access concerns while de‑emphasizing technical specifics.
Right outlets stress concreteness—citing "renderings" and procedural steps—to portray the project as formal progress and a surveillance risk .
Center coverage supplies technical details—"33,000‑square‑foot" size and "operating by July 2028"—omitted on the left.
Pivots are renderings/agenda inclusion, size and timeline, and liberty‑versus‑administration priorities; all agree the proposal exists and is advancing, exposing deeper security‑vs‑liberty divides.
summary: On Friday, the White House proposed building an underground screening center beneath Sherman Park, with plans on a preliminary agenda for the April 2 meeting of the National Capital Planning Commission.

The project follows the East Wing demolition last fall, with officials framing it as the latest step in President Donald Trump's overhaul of the White House grounds where trailer-type screening structures long stood.

Renderings show a 33,000-square-foot facility beneath Sherman Park that preserves the Union Gen. William Tecumseh Sherman monument and adds seven lanes to replace lines on 15th Street at the corner of Lafayette Park.

On Friday, the commission's agenda includes a debate and vote on a 33,000-square-foot replacement where the East Wing stood, with construction possibly starting in August and aiming for July 2028 operation.

The National Park Service and partners note the project involves the Executive Office of the President, the U.S. Secret Service, and National Park Service beneath Sherman Park southeast of the White House.
title: The White House wants to build an underground center to provide security screening for visitors

bias: Left coverage repeatedly frames the outcome as a failure—using phrases like "didn't work that well" and "intense early onslaught"—to emphasize systemic causes and demand accountability; right coverage also uses "didn't work that well" but in terse, skeptical reports that de-emphasize CDC benchmarks or strain specifics; center coverage uses CDC data and context, citing the 40–60% benchmark and noting low rates this decade.

Pivotal dividing lines are whether to quantify effectiveness, name the new strain, or report hospitalizations and guidance; all agree the season is winding down and vaccines underperformed, reflecting ideological splits over trust, context, and culpability.
summary: On Mar 13, 2026, CDC data showed this season's vaccines were around 25% to 30% effective for adults, marking one of the lowest rates in two decades.
The new A H3N2 subclade K exploded early winter and was not well matched to this season's vaccine, a mismatch public-health bodies noted as likely reducing protection.
Vaccination coverage data show about 85% were not fully vaccinated, and 48% of U.S. kids vaccinated last month were about 40% less likely to need medical treatment.
CDC estimates show a heavy toll this season, with at least 27 million illnesses, 350,000 hospitalizations, 22,000 deaths, at least 101 child deaths, and flu visits declining to 16 states last week.
Work is already underway for next winter's flu season, with experts including Dr. William Schaffner advising the public that flu shots can prevent severe illness and death.
title: Flu vaccines didn't work that well in the US, officials find

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as punitive and personal—using terms like "bar/ban," "face ban," and "to weigh barring"—portraying WADA as targeting the president; center outlets de-emphasize personalization and frame it as procedural, using "maneuver," "refusal," "unpaid dues" and noting an executive-committee agenda.
Right-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized in these materials.

Pivotal dividing lines are accountability versus bureaucratic dispute and the missing owed-amounts and legal-feasibility analysis that intensify partisan readings; both perspectives nonetheless report WADA is considering rule changes tied to "unpaid dues," reflecting wider ideological priorities—accountability versus institutional governance.
summary: The World Anti-Doping Agency is considering barring President Trump and U.S. officials from attending the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics and 2026 World Cup due to unpaid U.S. government dues.
The proposal aims to sanction countries that do not pay their WADA dues and would exclude government representatives from major sporting events.
U.S. officials have protested WADA's handling of cases involving Chinese athletes and argued the agency lacks transparency, while WADA says the proposal is unrelated to the U.S. specifically.
title: WADA to weigh barring Trump, US officials from LA Olympics and possibly World Cup over unpaid dues

bias: Coverage splits along two narratives: left outlets emphasize institutional failure—using "cuts" and "strained," linking the spike to "war with Iran" and a "far‑right" protest—framing danger as a policy consequence; right outlets foreground Islamist motives and identities—highlighting "inspired by the Islamic State," "naturalized citizen from Lebanon," and "Allahu akbar"—framing danger as an ideological/immigration threat and urging accountability.
Right de-emphasized "cuts" while left de-emphasized attackers' origins.
Rhetorically, left uses systemic critique; right uses threat amplification and identity markers; center largely synthesizes both.
Both agree the threat is elevated and tied to the Iran backdrop, reflecting broader ideological and geopolitical divides.
summary: This past week, the FBI warned law enforcement about an unverified Iranian aspiration to conduct a drone attack against California amid three violence incidents across the U.S.
Amid last week's staffing changes, FBI and Justice Department senior officials warn that experienced counterterrorism staff have been removed, weakening capacity against Iran-linked threats.
On Thursday, March 12, 2026, police responded outside Old Dominion University after reports of an active shooter, with officials saying 'Allahu akbar' was yelled before the attacker was killed.
Officials warned expertise gaps while agencies said the Justice Department's National Security Division remains focused, despite recent personnel losses, according to former officials.
Given past plotting, Iran has targeted U.S. figures and used proxies, while lone actors radicalized online remain a lethal, unpredictable threat to the homeland.
title: US faces elevated terrorism threats against backdrop of Iran war and cuts at FBI, Justice Department

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the hearing as adversarial—using words like "grill/grilled" and spotlighting lawmakers' questioning about contacts with federal investigators to stress accountability—while right-leaning pieces adopt a terse, matter-of-fact tone, emphasizing Wade "stood behind" the prosecution and de-emphasizing coordination detail; center reports add procedural context and record Wade's denial as "absolute fiction.

The pivotal dividing lines are alleged White House coordination and investigator contacts, which elicit sharply different frames; both sides nonetheless report Wade testified and affirmed support for the prosecution, reflecting broader ideological splits over oversight and institutional trust.
summary: On Friday, Nathan Wade testified before a subcommittee in Atlanta, reading an opening statement that barred discussion of his relationship with Fani Willis .

The panel was created in January 2024 to examine Willis' hiring and alleged romance, which an appeals court said created an 'appearance of impropriety' and led to the case dismissal on November 26, 2026.
Wade said he could not recall when trips or calls happened, who he met, or what was discussed, but defended the investigation, stating 'she led us, I led the team and we did the work.'
Only Dolezal and Cowsert attended Friday’s subcommittee meeting, and Dolezal said Wade’s testimony raised questions about coordination to 'get Trump.'

With 2026 statewide campaigns underway, critics say the committee's scope expansion and little new evidence make the probe politically motivated, while legal-fee disputes continue. Wade's lawyer argued the hearings are partisan.
title: State lawmakers grill former special prosecutor Nathan Wade over Georgia Trump election case

summary: On Wednesday, the University of Maine Faculty Senate endorsed eliminating the master of arts in teaching Spanish and suspending the medical laboratory sciences bachelor's for three years.
Budget documents show low enrollment in the master's in teaching Spanish and a 7% budget cut amid a broader review, University of Maine System strategic planning document states.

The faculty committee forwarded the proposals to the provost and president, with university officials stating no faculty will be eliminated and students can complete degrees with required courses or substitutions.

Samantha Warren said the University of Maine System Board of Trustees will likely discuss the proposals at its next full meeting in May, and a full budget proposal for the 2027 fiscal year will be made public next week.

Across the University of Maine System, administrators have already approved system-wide program suspensions and eliminations despite criticism, with enrollment highest since 2021 even as next-year acceptances remain uncertain.
title: UMaine poised to end Spanish degree, suspend medical lab degree

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the rescue with repetitive action phrasing and de-emphasizes precise injury details; right-leaning pieces valorize responders with words like 'hoists' and 'to safety', add a distance , and hedge the condition as 'initial reports'; centrist copy reports the 'broken leg' without the right's qualifier.

Key divides are injury specificity, geographic context, and provisional wording; all sides agree a National Guard helicopter rescued a pinned logger in steep, rugged terrain per wire-service reporting—differences that echo broader ideological and cultural priorities.
summary: On Thursday, an Oregon Army National Guard HH-60 Black Hawk hoisted an injured logger to safety in Mount Hood National Forest after a falling tree pinned his leg about 60 miles southeast of Portland.
Steep, rugged terrain and downed trees made ground extraction dangerous, forcing the Estacada Fire District to call for aerial support after civilian air ambulances could not safely reach the site.

Estacada Fire District paramedic Tony Hadeed, a former Army National Guard flight medic, coordinated the operation while responders stabilized the logger before he was hoisted about 200 feet into the HH-60 Black Hawk.
Following the hoist, the logger was brought to a local hospital and reported in stable condition, Estacada Battalion Chief Matt Aalto told The Associated Press.

The operation underscores the essential role of specialized hoist-capable aircraft in Oregon's remote terrain; without the Oregon Army National Guard's unique equipment, rugged conditions in Mount Hood National Forest would have significantly complicated medical transport.
title: Oregon Army National Guard Hoists Injured Logger to Safety in Mount Hood National Forest

summary: In a letter released Friday, conductor Juanjo Mena said he will retire this year because his Alzheimer’s disease has progressed and this year’s concerts will be the last he conducts.
At age 60, Mena said he first disclosed his diagnosis in January 2025 and that his situation has changed since then, prompting important decisions.
Notably, Mena's conducting career has spanned 40 years, including roles as artistic director and principal conductor of the Bilbao Symphony Orchestra and chief conductor of the BBC Philharmonic .
IMG Artists said Mena will still conduct select performances, including the Barcelona Symphony Orchestra on March 21 and 22, and he asked that upcoming farewell concerts be celebrations.

His retirement this year closes an active chapter and will require programming and leadership changes at the Bilbao Symphony Orchestra, BBC Philharmonic, Cincinnati May Festival, and major guest orchestras.
title: Conductor Juanjo Mena, former Cincinnati festival head, to retire because of Alzheimer’s disease

summary: On March 11, 2026, pro‑Iranian hackers claimed responsibility for a cyberattack on Stryker, targeting sites in the Middle East as their campaign stretches into the U.S. during the war.
In recent years, Iran's cyber apparatus and allied hacking groups have expanded operations to wear down the U.S. war effort and strain cyber resources.
Researchers found hackers targeted cameras and regional datacenters, and discussed plans on Telegram and message boards, aiming to improve missile targeting and disrupt infrastructure.
Critical infrastructure such as hospitals, ports, water plants, power stations and railways face heightened risk, with Kevin Mandia warning, "Something is going to happen because the gloves are off."
Experts warn allied states could widen the campaign as CrowdStrike detected a surge including Z‑Pentest, and Polish authorities investigate a nuclear research facility attack with possible Iran ties.
title: Iran-linked hackers take aim at US and other targets, raising risk of cyberattacks during war

bias: Left outlets emphasize wealthy backers, using terms like "pro‑billionaire" and "billionaire‑backed" and spotlight the $5 inducement to frame a populist critique of elite influence and note campaigns blaming outside firms; right outlets foreground integrity and criminality, using phrases such as "petition fraud," "State Investigation Launched," and highlight collectors providing names/addresses to portray deliberate wrongdoing; center coverage is more descriptive—"launches probe," "video shows," and labels one effort "tech‑backed" while outlining legal questions.
Dividing lines include sponsor attribution, blame assignment, and scale/legal consequences; all agree a video prompted a state investigation.
summary: On Friday, California election officials announced an investigation into whether signature collectors in San Francisco illegally paid people to sign ballot petitions using false names.
A video surfaced Monday showing a sign that says "Sign petition for $5" with a worker instructing individuals to provide specific names and addresses for the forms.
Building a Better California, a committee started by business leaders including Google co-founder Sergey Brin who gave $20 million, is funding the measure to fight a proposed tax on billionaires.
Molly Weedn, a spokesperson for the effort, said the collectors were not directly working for the campaign, which notified authorities as soon as the video surfaced.
Under California law, offering money in exchange for signatures is illegal; officials confirmed that petitions containing forged names will not be verified or counted.
title: California launches probe after video shows petition gatherers offering money for signatures
Facing rockets, Arabs in northern Israel fume over lack of shelters

bias: In the supplied analyses, right-leaning outlets frame the campaign as decisive kinetic success—using words like "destroyed," "eliminated," "struck," and citing "200 targets" and "1,200 deaths"—employing triumphant, security-first rhetoric and political signaling; center coverage foregrounds a Pentagon -sourced cumulative figure "more than 15,000" and labels it a "war," emphasizing scale and official attribution rather than battlefield triumphalism.
Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized in the sample.

Pivotal divides include casualty counts, independent verification, and warnings to Iran’s leadership, while both sides agree the U.S. and Israel are conducting strikes, reflecting broader ideological and geopolitical rifts.
summary: The Israeli Air Force struck over 200 targets in western and central Iran within 24 hours, hitting ballistic missile launchers, defense systems, and combat production sites as part of Operation Roar of the Lion to reduce threats to Israel.

The Israel Defence Forces claimed to have destroyed the Taleghan compound, used by Iran for nuclear weapons development during the AMAD project, and eliminated several key commanders linked to Hezbollah and the Iranian regime, including Abu Dharr Mohammadi, Ali Muslim Tabaja, and Abu Ali Riyan.
IDF reported eliminating over 100 fighters and destroying more than 60 command and control centers associated with Hezbollah's Radwan Force.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu warned Iran's new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei, calling him a puppet of the Revolutionary Guard and hinting at possible future actions against terrorist organizations.
title: US, Israel Hit More than 15,000 Targets in Iran War: Pentagon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the cancellation as a moral failing, foregrounding human-rights terms like "torture camp" and "notorious" and portraying the dropping of indictments against five soldiers as evidence of impunity; right-leaning narratives emphasize procedural defenses—"ambiguous footage," "evidentiary difficulties," "dropped charges"—portraying the outcome as legally justified and accusing critics of smear or leak-driven hysteria while noting the detainee's release to Gaza complicated prosecution.
Pivotal divides center on the leaked video, the detainee's release, and government rhetoric like "blood libel.
Center coverage de-emphasized moralizing and stressed legal review; all agree charges were dropped, video exists, and public reaction was intense.
summary: On March 12, 2026, the Israeli military announced it is dropping charges against five soldiers accused of sexually abusing a Palestinian detainee, according to The Associated Press.
At Sde Teiman, a detention facility for Palestinians rounded up from Gaza, a leaked Channel 12 video last year purported to show abuse, prompting fury and ultranationalist overruns.
Members of Israel’s far-right government called the investigation an affront to their service, and the uproar led to the resignation last year of the military’s top legal official amid the Gaza war.
Critics argue the decision reinforces long-standing accusations that Israel fails to hold soldiers accused of crimes against Palestinians, deepening domestic division.

Internationally, the story highlighted Israel’s claim that its forces act within military and international law, with Channel 12’s leaked footage and The Associated Press coverage drawing wider scrutiny.
title: Israeli Military Drops Charges Against Soldiers Charged with Abusing Palestinian Prisoner

bias: Left outlets frame the easing as enabling Moscow—using phrases like "hijack the global economy" and "boosted Moscow's ability to profit"—emphasizing moral critique and Ukraine-linked harm; right outlets cast it as an "Energy Crisis" with "Little Hope for Quick Peace," stressing economic pain, alarm and European annoyance.
Rhetorically, left leans accusatory and moralizing, right uses pessimistic, crisis-driven language; center coverage is more market-focused and de‑emphasizes moral framing.
Dividing lines include Ukrainian consequences and which sanctions were lifted; both agree sanctions eased and prices remain high.
summary: On Friday, March 13, 2026, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced a 30-day exemption for Russian oil deliveries loaded on tankers as of Thursday, following an earlier reprieve for refineries in India.

Recent market volatility surfaced when shipping disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries 20% of global oil, contributed to Brent crude briefly rising above $100 to $103.24 per barrel on Friday.

Simone Tagliapietra of Bruegel said 'In the short term this slightly increases available supply on the global market, which helps contain the current spike in oil prices', while analysts estimate about 125 million barrels are currently being shipped and Russia's daily earnings reach 510 million euros.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy criticized the easing, warning it could provide Russia about $10 billion for the war, while sanctions on Lukoil and Rosneft remain in place.
Analysts say the limited exemption does not change long-term Russian oil flows as Russia's use of a 'shadow fleet' and prices over $80 per barrel have boosted revenues by 14%.
title: US eases some Russian oil sanctions but crude prices stay high
House Republicans threaten to oppose Senate bills until SAVE America Act passes
